raw_case:
A rare case of extra-digital glomus tumor of the thigh


Introduction

Glomus tumors are rare benign tumors arising from glomus bodies that are responsible for thermoregulatory control. Their typical location is the subungual area of the digits, and extra-digital glomus tumors are very rare, leading to misdiagnosis and delayed treatment due to the absence of typical symptoms.

Case

Here, we report the case of a 49 years old male patient with a long history of localized right thigh pain who was found to have an extra-digital glomus tumor of the thigh after surgical excision.

Discussion

A comprehensive physical examination, detailed medical history, in depth imaging and early surgical excision upon clinical suspicion may prevent delayed or incorrect diagnosis. The treatment of glomus tumor is surgical excision providing immediate relief from pain, however if the lesion is not palpable, it can be easily missed or confusing with other diagnoses such as schwannoma, neuroma or arteriovenous malformation.

Conclusion

Glomus tumors of the thigh represent an exceptional location for extra digital glomus tumors. The aim of this report was to make the surgical community more aware of this entity to prevent delayed treatment and misdiagnosis. Glomus tumor should be kept in mind in the differential diagnosis of all painful subcutaneous lesions.

Highlights

•Glomus tumors are benign neoplasms containing cells from the glomus apparatus which is responsible for thermoregulatory control. It is a rare tumors accounting for only 1-2% of all soft tissue tumors that usually presents as a painful subcutaneous nodule formation located on the subungual area of the digits.•Extra-digital glomus tumors are a very rare presentation and the location in the thigh is exceptional with only a few cases reported in the literature.•The treatment of glomus tumor is surgical excision providing immediate relief from pain, however if the lesion is not palpable, it can be easily missed or confusing with other diagnoses such as schwannoma, neuroma or arteriovenous malformation.•Glomus tumor should be kept in mind in the differential diagnosis of all painful subcutaneous lesions.1

Introduction

Glomus tumors are benign neoplasms containing cells from the glomus apparatus which is responsible for thermoregulatory control [1]. It is a rare tumors accounting for only 1–2 % of all soft tissue tumors that usually presents as a painful subcutaneous nodule formation located on the subungual area of the digits [2]. Extra-digital glomus tumor's locations are even rarer leading to misdiagnosis and delayed treatment due to the physician's lack of awareness and low level of suspicion [3]. The work has been reported in line with the SCARE criteria [4].We report here the case of a 49 years old male patient with a long history of localized right thigh pain found to have an extra-digital glomus tumor of the thigh after surgical excision.2

Case

A 49-year-old male patient presented with localized pain in the medial aspect of the right thigh. The pain started insidiously 5 years ago, exacerbated by movement or upon palpation and not relieved by anti-inflammatory. As past medical history patient is known to have dyslipidemia and negative past surgical history. On Physical exam there were any palpable mass on the right thigh. The overlying skin was normal without any evidence of inflammation. Range of motion and neurological examination of the right lower limb were normal. Patient denied any traumatic injury to the thigh.The patient saw multiple physicians and multiple imaging including ultrasound and Magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) were done without final diagnosis. MRI done two years ago showed an enhancing 5 mm nodule in the subcutaneous fat of the medial distal thigh showing a well-defined border and without infiltration of the adjacent muscles. The subcutaneous location and the stable size since prior examination were suggestive of benign etiology.When patient presented to our care, repeat imaging and laboratory studies were done. Biology, including Complete blood count, C-reactive protein and erythrocyte sedimentation rate were within normal limits. Echo Doppler showed at the level of the painful area on the right medial thigh, a non-specific non-inflammatory granulomatous mass of 4 × 2mm, not vascularized and 5 mm deep from the skin. Thigh MRI with gadolinium showed a 4 × 2 mm oval-shaped nodule located in the subcutaneous area far from the dermis on the median right thigh and shows 2 tiny vessels artery and vein traversing it. Findings were suspicious of angioma or arteriovenous malformation, but schwannoma or neuroma can't be ruled out (Fig. 1).Facing diagnosis difficulties, an excision of the mass was performed. The operation was done under local anesthesia with lidocaine injection. Extensive dissection was performed following the site of the nodule seen on MRI till reaching the mass that was excised en bloc with negative macroscopic margins. The mass was firm and differentiated from the fat by its tissue density (Fig. 2). Pathological examination of the specimen revealed mostly normal mature adipose, in the center of which there was an 8 mm solid well circumscribed encapsulated nodule. The nodule was composed of sheets of uniform cells, interrupted by vessels of variable size. The neoplastic cells exhibited round nuclei with regular contour and well-defined outlines (Fig. 3). Necrosis and mitotic figures were absent. The neoplastic cells showed strong expression of Smooth Muscle Actin (SMA). EMA and S-100 stains were Negative. CD34 immunohistochemical stain highlighted the endothelial cells of the vessels that were surrounded by the neoplastic cells (Fig. 4).Postoperatively, the patient was immediately relieved from pain and discharged home on the same day. Follow up at 2 months post operatively, the patient was pain free.3

Discussion

Glomus bodies, a neuro-myo-arterial structure located at the dermo-hypodermic junction, are the source of glomus tumors, which are uncommon benign tumors. The glomus bodies' function is to control blood flow to the skin in response to variations in body temperature. Less than 1 % of all soft tissue cancers are globus tumors [2]. These can be categorized as extra-digital or digital/subungual, solitary or numerous, sporadic or familial lesions [5]. Although glomus bodies are present all over the body, they are most heavily concentrated in the hands, feet, and palms, which explains why the subungual region and lateral portions of the fingers are where they are most frequently found [6]. There are extremely few examples of extra-digital glomus tumors documented in the literature, and the location in the thigh is unusual [7]. Only five out of the 56 individuals with extra-digital glomus tumors had the tumor located in their thighs [7]. Because of the deep placement, tiny tumor size, and lack of particular symptoms, it is frequently misdiagnosed and treated incorrectly [6].Hand glomus tumors typically exhibit the traditional symptoms of pain and cold intolerance. The lack of normal symptoms makes extra-digital glomus tumor diagnosis more challenging. In their investigation of five individuals with extra-digital glomus tumors, Temiz et al. noted that a consistent finding was a purple subcutaneous lump that felt quite painful when palpated [6]. Lee et al. compared 42 individuals with extra-digital glomus tumors to 110 patients with digital glomus tumors and discovered that single purplish papules were the most typical clinical manifestation of extra-digital glomus tumors. When it came to extra-digital glomus tumors, the male to female ratio was 4:1, however in digital glomus tumors, the female predominance was 2:1 [7]. Our patient was suffering from pain that is exacerbated by movement and tenderness to palpation without hypersensitivity to cold. And the mass had no skin discoloration and wasn't palpable on physical exam so it was difficult to suspect a glomus tumor at this stage.It has been proposed that MRI and ultrasound imaging can help with glomus tumor diagnosis. A generic, well-demarcated, rounded, hypoechoic mass that is hypervascularized on doppler is shown on ultrasound imaging. MRI especially in high resolution represents the most sensible and specific tool distinguishing between the tumor itself and the normal tissue. When gadolinium is injected, magnetic resonance imaging reveals a low signal intensity on T1 weighted pictures and a high signal intensity on T2 weighted images, particularly around the edge. After histological analysis, complete excisional biopsy is still the most effective method for reaching a definitive diagnosis [3].A few clinical characteristics, such as big size, deep soft tissue or visceral location, infiltrative growth pattern, or multicentricity, might arouse the suspicion of a malignant glomus tumor, albeit the conversion to a malignant tumor is extremely rare—less than 1 % of instances have been recorded to occur [8,9].The treatment of glomus tumor is surgical excision providing immediate relief from pain, however if the lesion is not palpable, it can be easily missed or confusing with other diagnoses such as schwannoma, neuroma or arteriovenous malformation [[1], [2], [3], [4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9]]. Small lesions can be treated with laser ablation using argon or carbon dioxide lasers, sclerotherapy using sodium tetradecyl or hypertonic saline solution [[4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9]]. Recurrence after surgical resection is infrequent rates around 12 % in different publications and could be related to inadequate surgical excision or multiple primary tumors [[3], [4], [5], [6], [7], [8], [9], [10], [11]].4

Conclusion

Glomus tumors of the thigh represent an exceptional location of extra-digital glomus tumors. They usually present with pain and localized tenderness. The diagnosis is frequently delayed due to lack of awareness among physicians and low level of suspicion. A comprehensive physical examination, detailed medical history, in depth imaging and early surgical excision upon clinical suspicion may prevent delayed or incorrect diagnosis. Glomus tumor should be kept in mind in the differential diagnosis of all painful subcutaneous lesions.

Informed consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient for publication and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

Case report approved for publishing by ethical committee at Saint George hospital University Medical Center.

Funding

None.

Author contribution

Camil Chouairy MD: supervision, validation, project administration, conception of the work, revising the work critically for important intellectual content and final approval of the version to be published project administration, Souad Ghattas MD: first Author, conception of the work, design of the work, review of literature, draft manuscript, revising the work critically for important intellectual content, final approval of the version to be published, Hani Maalouf MD: review of literature, draft manuscript, revising the work critically for important electual content, final approval of the version to be published, Ahmad Younes MD: conception of the work, design of the work,review of literature, draft manuscript, revising the work critically for important electual content, final approval of version to be published, Jad El Bitar MD: review of literature, draft manuscript, Mansour El Khoury MD: supervision, validation, project administration, conception of the work, revising the work critically for important intellectual content, final approval of the version to be published project administration and corresponding Author.

Guarantor

Souad Ghattas.

Research registration number

N/A.

Conflict of interest statement

The authors report no conflict of interest.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 49-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Localized right thigh pain
- **History of Present Illness:** Gradual onset of localized pain in the medial aspect of the right thigh lasting 5 years. The pain worsened with movement and palpation but was not relieved by anti-inflammatory medications.
- **Past Medical History:** Dyslipidemia
- **Personal History:** None
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:** No palpable mass; normal overlying skin; no inflammation or skin discoloration; normal range of motion and neurological exam of the right lower limb.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - MRI (initial): 5mm well-defined subcutaneous nodule in the medial distal thigh with stable size; benign-appearing.
  - Repeat Imaging/Tests: Echo Doppler showed a non-specific, non-inflammatory, non-vascularized subcutaneous granulomatous mass (4x2mm), 5mm deep. MRI with gadolinium revealed a 4x2mm oval nodule in subcutaneous fat with two traversing vessels (artery and vein), suspicious for angioma or arteriovenous malformation but could not exclude schwannoma or neuroma.
  - Histopathology: Encapsulated solid tumor with sheets of uniform cells and vessels, no necrosis or mitotic figures. Smooth Muscle Actin (SMA)-positive; EMA and S-100-negative; CD34 emphasized endothelial cells of surrounding vessels.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial Considerations:**
  - **Angioma or Arteriovenous Malformation (AVM):** Considered due to MRI findings of vessels traversing the nodule. Excluded after histopathology showed features inconsistent with AVM or angioma.
  - **Schwannoma or Neuroma:** Considered due to MRI results suggesting benign nerve-based etiology. Excluded as immunohistochemical markers (S-100 negative) and histological appearance were inconsistent.
  - **Granulomatous Mass:** Suggested by Doppler echo but excluded due to histopathology indicating encapsulated tumor without granulomatous inflammation.
  - **Malignant Neoplasm:** Excluded as no evidence of necrosis, infiltrative growth, or mitotic activity was observed.
- Final consideration was an **Extra-digital Glomus Tumor**, aligning well with histopathological findings (SMA positivity, vascular involvement) despite the unusual thigh location. Surgical excision provided immediate symptom relief, further supporting this diagnosis.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Extra-digital glomus tumor of the right thigh.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Extra-digital glomus tumor of the right thigh.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 49-year-old man with a 5-year history of right medial thigh pain that's worse with movement and palpation. Anti-inflammatories didn't help. His past medical history includes dyslipidemia, but nothing else significant.

First, the initial MRI showed a 5mm subcutaneous nodule that's well-defined and benign-looking. The repeat imaging with Echo Doppler described a granulomatous mass, non-inflammatory and non-vascularized. Then, the MRI with gadolinium noted a small oval nodule with two vessels passing through, which made them think of angioma, AVM, schwannoma, or neuroma. But the histopathology is key here. The tumor is encapsulated, has uniform cells, no necrosis or mitoses. SMA-positive, EMA and S-100 negative, CD34 highlights the vessels.

Hmm. Let's start with the imaging. The nodule is in the subcutaneous fat. The presence of vessels could suggest vascular lesions like angioma or AVM. But the histopathology shows SMA positivity. SMA is a marker for smooth muscle, which is found in certain tumors like leiomyomas or perivascular tumors. But EMA is negative, which might rule out some neural or epithelial tumors. S-100 is negative, which makes schwannoma less likely since those are typically S-100 positive. CD34 is highlighting endothelial cells, so the vessels are prominent here.

The encapsulated nature and the uniform cells without necrosis or mitotic activity point towards a benign tumor. The SMA positivity in the tumor cells suggests smooth muscle differentiation. The absence of S-100 makes schwannoma or neuroma less likely. The CD34 is more about the vessels around the tumor, not the tumor cells themselves. So maybe a perivascular tumor?

Angioleiomyoma comes to mind. Those are benign tumors arising from smooth muscle in blood vessel walls. They are SMA positive, often present with pain, and can have vascular components. The histopathology here seems to fit: encapsulated, smooth muscle actin positive, vessels present. The pain could be due to the location and the contraction of smooth muscle in response to stimuli. Also, they are typically superficial, in subcutaneous tissue, which matches the imaging findings.

Other possibilities: A vascular lesion like angioma, but the SMA positivity in the tumor cells themselves would be more in line with smooth muscle rather than endothelial cells. Arteriovenous malformation is more of a tangle of vessels, and the histo here shows sheets of uniform cells. Schwannoma would have S-100, so that's out. Leiomyoma (cutaneous) is possible, but angioleiomyoma is a subtype that's more associated with vessels.

Putting it all together: The clinical presentation of chronic pain, the imaging showing a small nodule with vessels, and the histopathology pointing to a smooth muscle tumor with vascular elements. The SMA positivity and lack of other markers support angioleiomyoma. The pain is a common symptom, possibly due to contraction of the smooth muscle component. All other differentials don't fit as well as this.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient’s chronic right thigh pain localized to the medial aspect, exacerbated by movement/palpation, and unresponsive to anti-inflammatories suggests a structural lesion rather than inflammatory or degenerative pathology. The absence of systemic symptoms or neurological deficits narrows the focus to a localized soft tissue tumor.

<step 2> MRI and Echo Doppler findings describe a small, stable subcutaneous nodule with traversing vessels. While initial imaging raised possibilities of angioma, arteriovenous malformation (AVM), schwannoma, or neuroma, these were not fully conclusive. The benign appearance and lack of vascular inflammation argue against aggressive or infectious etiologies.

<step 3> Histopathology is pivotal: the tumor is encapsulated with uniform cells, no necrosis/mitoses (indicating benignity), SMA-positive cells (smooth muscle origin), and CD34 highlighting vascular endothelium. EMA/S-100 negativity excludes perineurioma (EMA+) and schwannoma/neuroma (S-100+). The combination of smooth muscle differentiation and vascular association points to a vascular smooth muscle tumor.

<step 4> Angioleiomyoma (vascular leiomyoma) aligns with the histopathological profile. These tumors arise from vascular smooth muscle, are SMA-positive, and often present with pain due to contractile properties. The encapsulated nature, vascular components, and absence of malignancy markers (e.g., mitoses) further support this diagnosis. Other differentials (angioma, AVM) lack the smooth muscle proliferation seen here.

### Answer:
Angioleiomyoma (vascular leiomyoma) of the subcutaneous tissue.
